topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay on the worn kitchen table, a stark white sheet mocking the gloom of the afternoon. My fingers traced the blurred lines of the genetic report, each name a foreign sound echoing in the silence of my small apartment. I stared at the percentages, the markers, the undeniable proof laid bare in alphanumeric code. A coldness seeped into my bones, a familiar chill that settled deep in my chest, making every breath feel heavy and shallow. I picked up the phone, my hand trembling so badly I almost dropped it. The sheer weight of this revelation felt like a physical pressure, crushing the air from my lungs. I didn't know how to process the sudden shift in my entire reality, only that the foundation I thought I stood on had just dissolved into dust.

[story 2]

He sat on the edge of the sofa, meticulously folding the paper into tiny, precise squares. The fluorescent light above hummed, amplifying the hollow echo in his own mind. Every line of the DNA results seemed to confirm a deep-seated fear: that he was fundamentally incomplete, a composite of parts that didn't truly belong together. He kept rubbing his temples, a restless, repetitive motion that betrayed the internal storm raging beneath his calm exterior. When his mother finally walked in, the casual warmth of her greeting felt like an intrusion, highlighting the chasm between who he perceived himself to be and the objective data presented on the page. He managed a small, tight nod, his throat suddenly constricted, unable to form a coherent sentence.

[story 3]

The laboratory results were just facts, cold and immutable, yet they ignited a furious, internal combustion. She looked at the screen, and a strange, nauseating wave washed over her. It wasn't just information; it was an erasure of her own narrative. She paced the length of the living room, the hardwood floor protesting softly beneath her hurried steps. Her hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white against the pale skin. When she finally stopped, leaning against the windowpane, the city lights below seemed distant and indifferent. A profound sense of displacement settled over her, a heavy cloak of unfamiliarity she hadn't asked for, and she felt utterly adrift.
